topicData.topic:
BlackRock-backed group commits $5 billion for Aligned Data Centers after completing acquisition
By Reuters
July 21, 20264:11 PM GMT+5:30Updated 57 mins ago



The BlackRock logo is seen outside of its offices in New York
The BlackRock logo is seen outside of its offices in New York City, U.S., October 17, 2016. REUTERS/Brendan McDermid/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab
July 21 (Reuters) - A consortium backed by BlackRock and Abu ‚ÄãDhabi-based fund MGX said on Tuesday ‚Äåit has committed an additional $5 billion in growth capital to Aligned Data Centers after closing ‚Äãits $40 billion acquisition of one of ‚Äãthe world's biggest data center operators.
The ‚ÅÝinvestor group agreed last year to ‚Äãbuy Aligned from Australian asset manager Macquarie ‚ÄãAsset Management (MQG.AX), opens new tab, as it seeks to secure computing capacity for AI.
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.


Founded in 2013, the Texas-based company builds ‚Äãand operates data centers for hyperscalers ‚Äãand cloud computing companies. It has 51 data ‚Äåcenter ‚ÅÝcampuses and more than 6.4 gigawatts of operational and planned capacity globally.
Andrew Schaap, chief executive of Aligned Data Centers since ‚Äã2017, will ‚Äãcontinue ‚ÅÝto lead the company.

topicData.angle: Write from the specific details and your honest reaction to this topic.

postText:
$40 billion to acquire. Then immediately another $5 billion on top. That's not a calculated investment thesis. That's urgency.

@BlackRock and MGX didn't close one of the biggest data center deals in history and then pause to review the numbers. They wrote another check the same week.

6.4 gigawatts. 51 campuses. These figures weren't in enterprise boardroom conversations three years ago. Now they're the language of competitive positioning.

Here's what I keep coming back to though. The organisations writing these cheques aren't the ones who will struggle most with what comes next. It's the 35,000-person logistics company or the facilities management firm that now has to figure out what to do with compute capacity that didn't exist when their IT strategy was written.

The infrastructure build is accelerating faster than most large organisations can absorb it. That gap is where the real risk sits.

Where are you seeing that tension show up in your organisation?

#EnterpriseAI #TechLeadership #DigitalTransformation
